title: Understanding Cash Flow Ratios in Bank Statement Analysis
meta: Cash flow ratios help lenders quantify repayment capacity, financial stability, and risk. Learn the most important ratios used in bank statement analysis and how to calculate them.
content:
Why Ratios Matter in Bank Statement Analysis

Raw numbers tell part of the story — someone earning $10,000/month sounds financially healthy. But if that same person has $9,500 in monthly obligations, their financial position is precarious. Ratios put raw numbers in context, making comparisons meaningful and risk assessment objective.

Debt Service Coverage Ratio (DSCR)

Formula: Net Operating Income ÷ Total Debt Service

What it measures: How many times over the borrower's income covers their debt payments. A DSCR of 1.5 means income is 1.5x the total debt obligations.

Interpretation:

  • Below 1.0: Income doesn't cover obligations — negative cash flow
  • 1.0–1.15: Technically covers obligations but no cushion
  • 1.25+: Comfortable coverage — most lenders' minimum for business loans
  • 2.0+: Very strong — significant cushion against income reduction

From bank statements: Calculate average monthly deposits (net of transfers and one-time items) as income. Identify all recurring debt payments as debt service.

Cash Flow-to-Income Ratio

Formula: Net Cash Flow ÷ Gross Income × 100

What it measures: The percentage of income that remains after all obligations, expressed as the free cash flow margin.

Interpretation:

  • Below 10%: Very thin margin — any income reduction or unexpected expense creates problems
  • 10–25%: Adequate — manageable buffer
  • 25%+: Healthy — significant discretionary cash available

NSF Rate

Formula: Number of NSF events ÷ Number of months reviewed

What it measures: Frequency of account overdrafts — a proxy for how often the borrower runs out of money before income arrives.

Interpretation:

  • 0: No financial stress indicator from this metric
  • 0.5–1.0 per month: Occasional stress — investigate
  • 2+ per month: Chronic cash flow problem — significant risk

Balance Stability Ratio

Formula: Lowest Monthly Balance ÷ Average Monthly Income

What it measures: How deep the borrower's financial cushion is at their lowest point in any given month relative to their income.

Interpretation:

  • Below 0.1 (balance drops to less than 10% of monthly income): Lives paycheck to paycheck
  • 0.25–0.5: Modest cushion
  • 1.0+: Maintains at least one full month of income as minimum balance — strong financial discipline

Income Stability Score

Formula: Lowest Monthly Deposit ÷ Highest Monthly Deposit

What it measures: Income variability — how consistent income is across the review period.

Interpretation:

  • Below 0.5: High variability — at least one month was less than half the best month
  • 0.7–0.9: Moderate variability — acceptable for self-employed borrowers
  • 0.9+: Very consistent — typical of salaried employees

Expense Ratio (for Business Accounts)

Formula: Total Withdrawals ÷ Total Deposits

What it measures: What percentage of income is consumed by expenses. For a business, this is the inverse of the profit margin.

Interpretation by industry:

  • Retail/distribution: 70–85% expense ratio is typical
  • Service businesses: 40–60% expense ratio
  • Professional services: 30–50% expense ratio

Putting Ratios Together

No single ratio tells the full story. A high DSCR combined with poor balance stability might indicate a high earner with terrible cash management habits. A modest DSCR combined with strong balance stability suggests a disciplined borrower who is financially prepared.

AI tools like StatementScrub calculate all these ratios automatically from any bank statement PDF, presenting them together in a comprehensive risk profile that gives lenders the complete picture in seconds.
